All of Israel mourned for Samuel, and they had reason. He prayed daily for them. Those who have hard hearts cannot grieve over the loss of faithful ministers; they do not feel the pain of losing those who have prayed for them and taught them the way of the Lord.

We would not know of Nabal if nothing had happened between him and David. Observe his name, Nabal, which means “A fool.” Riches make men appear great in the eyes of the world, but to one who sees things clearly, Nabal seemed very insignificant. He had no honor or honesty; he was rude, grumpy, and ill-tempered; evil in his actions, harsh and oppressive; a man who did not care what fraud and violence he used to get and save wealth. What little reason do we have to value the riches of this world when a churl like Nabal thrives, and a good man like David suffers want? David appealed to Nabal’s shepherds' kindnesses. Considering that David’s men were in distress, had debts, and were discontented, and there was a shortage of food, it was by careful management that they were kept from plundering. Nabal became angry, as greedy people are prone to do when asked for anything, thinking thus to cover one sin with another; and, by mistreating the poor, to excuse himself from helping them. But God will not be mocked in this way. Let this help us to bear insults and misrepresentations with patience and cheerfulness, and make us easy under them; it has often been the lot of excellent people. Nabal insisted greatly on the property he had in the provisions of his table. May he not do what he wants with his own? We are mistaken if we think we are absolute lords of what we have and may do as we please with it. No; we are but stewards, and must use it as we are directed, remembering that it is not our own, but His who entrusted us with it.

God is kind to the wicked and unthankful, so why should we not be? David decided to destroy Nabal and all that belonged to him. Is this your voice, O David? Has he been in the school of affliction for so long, where he should have learned patience, yet is so passionate? He is usually calm and considerate, but a few harsh words put him into such a rage that he seeks to destroy an entire family. What are the best of men when God leaves them to themselves, so they may know what is in their hearts? We need to pray, “Lord, lead us not into temptation!”

By a timely visit from Abigail, Nabal’s denial of David's request was atoned for. Her behavior was very submissive. Yielding pacifies great offenses. She put herself in the place of a penitent and a petitioner. She could not excuse her husband’s conduct. She does not rely on her own reasoning but on God’s grace to soften David, and expects that grace would work powerfully. She says that it was beneath him to take vengeance on so weak and despicable an enemy as Nabal, who, because he would do him no kindness, could also do him no harm. She foretells the glorious end of David's present troubles. God will preserve your life; therefore, it does not become you unjustly and unnecessarily to take the lives of any, especially those of the people of your God and Savior. Abigail offered this argument last, as very persuasive with so good a man; that the less he indulged his passion, the more he consulted his peace and the repose of his own conscience. Many have done things in a heat that they have wished undone again. The sweetness of revenge is soon turned into bitterness. When tempted to sin, we should consider how it will appear when we think upon it afterwards.

David gives God thanks for sending him this happy check in a sinful way. Whoever meets us with counsel, direction, comfort, caution, or seasonable reproof, we must see as sent by God. We ought to be very thankful for those happy providences which are the means of keeping us from sinning. Most people think it enough if they take reproof patiently; but few will take it thankfully, and commend those who give it, and accept it as a favor. The nearer we are to committing sin, the greater is the mercy of a seasonable restraint. Sinners are often most secure when most in danger. He was very drunk. A sign he was Nabal, a fool, who could not use plenty without abusing it; who could not be pleasant with his friends without making a beast of himself. There is no surer sign that a man has little wisdom, nor a surer way to destroy the little he has, than drinking to excess. Next morning, how he is changed! His heart overnight merry with wine, next morning heavy as a stone; so deceitful are carnal pleasures, so soon passes the laughter of the fool; the end of that mirth is heaviness. Drunkards are sad when they reflect upon their own folly. About ten days after, the Lord struck Nabal, and he died. David blessed God that he had been kept from killing Nabal. Worldly sorrow, mortified pride, and an affrighted conscience sometimes end the joys of the sensualist, and separate the covetous man from his wealth; but whatever the weapon, the Lord smites men with death when it pleases him.

Abigail believed that David would be king over Israel, and greatly esteemed his pious and excellent character. She deemed his proposal of marriage honorable and advantageous to her, despite his present difficulties. With great humility, and doubtless agreeably to the customs of those times, she consented, being willing to share his trials. Thus those who join themselves to Christ must be willing now to suffer with him, believing that hereafter they shall reign with him.
